1: The chapter opens with a genre designation: elle mas'ei bnei yisrael — 'these are the stages/journeys of the Israelites.' The Hebrew term massa (H4550, journey-stage, travel-camp) is the technical term for a stage in a march — each massa is one leg from one camp to the next. The itinerary genre was well-established in ANE administrative and military literature: Egyptian campaign annals (Thutmose III's Annals at Karnak), Assyrian royal campaign records, and Egyptian wisdom texts all preserve route-lists with stage-by-stage camp notations. Numbers 33 is Israel's own march document, fitting this ANE administrative genre.
2: 'By the LORD's command, Moses recorded their departures stage by stage.' Vayiktov moshe et-motzaeihem lemassa'eihem al-pi YHWH — Moses wrote their departures stage by stage at YHWH's command. This is a crucial meta-note: the list is not a later reconstruction but a contemporaneous written record commanded by YHWH. The claim to Mosaic authorship and divine authorization places the itinerary in the same category as the legal texts of Numbers — authoritative, written, preserved. Motzaeihem (their departures/goings-out) and massa'eihem (their stages) together emphasize Israel's essential character in this period: a moving, departing people.
3: 'They set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day — the day after the Passover — in full sight of all the Egyptians, with a high hand.' B'yad ramah — 'with a high hand' — an idiom of bold, powerful action under divine empowerment (cf. Exod 14:8; Deut 32:27). The departure is public and audacious: no hiding, no night escape, but a full-daylight march. Rameses is the most likely identification with Pi-Ramesses, the Nile Delta capital built by Ramesses II (13th century BCE), which Egyptian sources confirm was a major administrative center used as a military mustering point. The 15th of Nisan connects the departure explicitly to the Passover legislation of ch.28.
4: 'While the Egyptians were burying their firstborn, whom the LORD had struck down; the LORD had also executed judgments against their gods.' The juxtaposition is deliberate and theologically loaded: Israel marching out in triumph while Egypt buries its dead — the two peoples' trajectories at the moment of exodus are exactly reversed from their positions during the slavery period. Uvieloheihem asah YHWH shfatim — 'and on their gods YHWH executed judgments.' The plagues are reframed as a juridical act: YHWH adjudicating against the divine claims of Egypt's pantheon. Egypt's gods could not protect Egypt's firstborn; their powerlessness is the judgment.
5: The Israelites moved from Rameses and camped at Succoth. Succoth (H5523, booths/shelters) is the first stage — the staging area east of Rameses where the Israelites gathered before departing into the wilderness (Exod 12:37; 13:20). The name may indicate a place where field shelters or temporary structures were common, or it may be a topographic marker. Succoth appears in Egyptian sources as a site in the eastern Delta near the Wadi Tumilat — the corridor connecting Egypt to Sinai.
6: From Succoth to Etham — 'at the edge of the wilderness.' Etham marks the transition from the settled Delta world to the Sinai wilderness. The phrase biqtzeh hamidbar (at the edge/end of the wilderness) signals the beginning of the journey into the truly uninhabited zone. The traditional identification of Etham is uncertain; it may be near the modern Ismailia area, at the edge of the Sinai Peninsula.
7: From Etham to Pi-hahiroth (opposite Baal-zephon, before Migdol). This is the site of the sea-crossing (Exod 14:2): the Israelites camped here while Pharaoh's army approached. Pi-hahiroth may mean 'mouth of the canal/gorge' in Egyptian. Baal-zephon was likely an Egyptian cult site for the Canaanite storm-deity Baal associated with the north — a divine rival to YHWH at the very site of the sea-crossing. YHWH's victory at the Sea occurred in the shadow of a Baal-shrine, making the divine contest explicit.
8: From Hahiroth through the middle of the sea — b'tokh hayam — into the wilderness of Etham, three days' journey to Marah. The crossing of the sea is noted with minimal drama in the itinerary: it is one stage among many. The administrative brevity of the itinerary form gives the crossing the same grammatical weight as any other stage — but the reader knows that this stage contains the defining event of the exodus. The wilderness of Shur (Exod 15:22) is here called the wilderness of Etham — possibly a broader geographic designation encompassing the same area.
9: From Marah to Elim — 'where there were twelve springs and seventy palms.' Elim provides the only detailed campsite description in the entire itinerary (apart from the sea-crossing note of v.8 and Aaron's death at v.38-39). The twelve springs and seventy palms have invited symbolic reading: twelve springs for twelve tribes (or the twelve months), seventy palms for the seventy elders of Israel or the seventy nations of Gen 10. Whether the symbolism is intentional or retrospectively applied, the oasis is remembered by its extraordinary provision — water and shade in the wilderness, a prefiguration of YHWH's abundant provision throughout the journey.
10: From Elim the Israelites camped by the Red Sea. This campsite is mentioned only in the itinerary — it does not appear in the Exodus narrative. The Red Sea camp between Elim and the wilderness of Sin suggests that the route skirted the Sinai coast. The Red Sea/Sea of Reeds (yam suf) appears at multiple points in the wilderness itinerary, suggesting a more complex coastal route than the simplified Exodus narrative implies.
11: From the Red Sea to the wilderness of Sin. The wilderness of Sin (distinct from the wilderness of Zin where Kadesh is located) is the southern Sinai steppe between Elim and Sinai. Exod 16 records the manna provision in the wilderness of Sin — the community's grumbling about food was addressed with bread from heaven. The itinerary records the geographical stages without the narrative content; the reader must connect the place-names to their associated stories.
12: From the wilderness of Sin to Dophkah. Dophkah appears only here and in v.13 — it is not mentioned in Exodus and its location is unknown. Some scholars have proposed identification with a site near the copper-mining region of Serabit el-Khadim in Sinai, where Egyptian turquoise and copper mines were operated. If correct, the Israelites passed through or near an Egyptian industrial zone during their wilderness journey.
13: From Dophkah to Alush. Alush also appears only in this itinerary and cannot be identified with certainty. The string of otherwise unattested place-names in vv.12-35 reflects the itinerary's character as a working march document: it records every stage, not only the theologically significant ones. The unknown sites are a reminder that the wilderness period was a real, years-long journey across a real landscape whose full details have not been preserved beyond this single list.
14: From Alush to Rephidim — 'and there was no water for the people to drink.' Rephidim is the only campsite in this section given a descriptive note (besides Elim). The note echoes Exod 17:1-7: Moses struck the rock at Horeb and water came out. Rephidim was also the site of the battle with Amalek (Exod 17:8-16). The itinerary's brief annotation — 'no water to drink' — is a compressed narrative reference to one of the wilderness period's most dramatic confrontations with both human need and divine provision.
15: From Rephidim to the wilderness of Sinai. The wilderness of Sinai is the campsite for the Sinai revelation — the giving of the Law, the building of the tabernacle, the entire central section of Exodus and Leviticus. Israel camped at Sinai for an extended period; Numbers 1:1 begins 'in the wilderness of Sinai' on the first day of the second month of the second year. The wilderness of Sinai is the theological center of the itinerary: everything before it is approach; everything after it is departure.
16: From the wilderness of Sinai to Kibroth-hattaavah — 'graves of craving.' Kibroth-hattaavah is the named site of the quail incident (Num 11:31-34): the people craved meat, YHWH sent quail in such abundance it became a plague, and those who craved were buried there. The place-name encodes the narrative: the graves of craving mark the spot where desire became judgment. It is the first stage of departure from Sinai, and it is immediately marked by a crisis.
17: From Kibroth-hattaavah to Hazeroth. Hazeroth is where Miriam and Aaron challenged Moses's authority (Num 12:1-16) — Miriam was struck with leprosy and Israel waited seven days for her recovery. The stage-name may mean 'enclosures' or 'settlements.' Two successive crises (Kibroth-hattaavah, Hazeroth) mark the departure from Sinai as a period of testing: the community's character is being revealed in the journey away from the place of revelation.
18: From Hazeroth to Rithmah. Rithmah may mean 'place of broom plants' (the rothem broom is common in the Sinai). The site cannot be identified with certainty. Some rabbinic interpreters connect Rithmah to Kadesh-barnea (where the spies were sent out), but this identification is not supported by the text itself. The itinerary continues recording stages whose content we do not know.
19: From Rithmah to Rimmon-perez. The name means 'pomegranate of the breach/gap' — possibly a topographic description of a cleft or ravine marked by pomegranate growth. Like most of the mid-itinerary stages, Rimmon-perez is unknown outside this list. The monotony of the march is itself a theological datum: the wilderness years involved not only the dramatic crises of Korah or the quail but hundreds of days of ordinary marching through featureless terrain.
20: From Rimmon-perez to Libnah. Libnah means 'white' — possibly a reference to white chalk or limestone terrain. A different Libnah appears in Canaan (Josh 10:29; 2 Kgs 8:22) as a Canaanite city later assigned to Judah. The wilderness Libnah is distinct. The wilderness period's 40 years produced place-names that are remembered nowhere else — the geography of divine faithfulness in the unseen years.
21: From Libnah to Rissah. Rissah appears only here and in v.22 — unknown outside the itinerary. The name may mean 'ruin' or 'dew,' but etymological speculation cannot supply historical content. The itinerary's value for these unknown stages is not informational but theological: it insists that every stage of the journey was significant enough to record, that YHWH's guidance covered every unnamed march-leg, not only the dramatic ones.
22: From Rissah to Kehelathah. The name may mean 'assembly/gathering,' possibly recalling a gathering of the community at this location. The Israelites' life in the wilderness was structured by assembly — the community gathered regularly at the tent of meeting (Num 10:3-7). Even the wilderness march involved ongoing communal formation, not just physical relocation.
23: From Kehelathah to Mount Shepher. A mountain stage — Mount Shepher ('beauty/brightness') was a significant enough landmark to name in the itinerary. Mountains in the wilderness itinerary function as orienting landmarks: Sinai, Horeb, Hor, Shepher. The wilderness of Sinai is not featureless but textured with peaks and passes. The Israelites' journey through the Sinai interior followed a mountain route with identifiable high points.
24: From Mount Shepher to Haradah. The name Haradah may mean 'terror' or 'trembling' — possibly a memory of fear at this campsite, or simply a topographic description. The wilderness journey included places of fear as well as places of provision: the landscape of the 40 years was emotionally and spiritually complex, not merely physically arduous.
25: From Haradah to Makheloth. Makheloth may mean 'assemblies/gatherings' — another assembly-memory embedded in a place-name. The route through central Sinai and into the Transjordan approaches involved ongoing community formation: the wilderness was not a period of spiritual dormancy but of covenantal constitution through shared experience of both hardship and provision.
26: From Makheloth to Tahath. Tahath means 'station, place below' — possibly a valley campsite after the mountain stages. The alternation between mountain stages (Shepher) and lower terrain (Tahath) reflects the actual topography of the Sinai interior, where routes traverse wadis and plateaux alternately.
27: From Tahath to Terah. Terah is also the name of Abraham's father (Gen 11:24-32). Whether the connection is etymological coincidence or deliberate narrative echo cannot be determined, but the name's appearance in the itinerary is striking: the patriarch who brought the family to the edge of Canaan and stopped (Gen 11:31-32) is mirrored in the wilderness generation who also stopped short of the goal. The itinerary's anonymous march includes the weight of prior covenantal history in its toponymy.
28: From Terah to Mithkah. Mithkah may mean 'sweetness' — possibly a campsite with sweet water, echoing the Marah/bitterness → sweetness reversal of Exod 15:23-25. The wilderness journey included both bitter and sweet waters; the names of the camps preserve the experiential memory of provision and hardship in compressed form.
29: From Mithkah to Hashmonah. Hashmonah may mean 'fatness/richness' — possibly naming a well-provisioned campsite. The variation in campsite-name meanings (terror, sweetness, richness, assembly) suggests that the place-names encode the community's experiential memory of each location: what happened there, what the land provided or withheld, what the people felt.
30: From Hashmonah to Moseroth. Some ancient and rabbinic traditions identify Moseroth with the site of Aaron's death (Deut 10:6 says Aaron died at Moserah). If so, the itinerary's placement here (before Mount Hor, v.37) and Deuteronomy's sequence may reflect a different arrangement of the same events, or the traditions refer to different events at a related site. The textual tension is unresolved but suggests the wilderness itinerary preserves an independent tradition about Aaron's final stages.
31: From Moseroth to Bene-jaakan. Bene-jaakan means 'sons of Jaakan' — the name of an Horite/Edomite clan (Gen 36:27; 1 Chr 1:42). The presence of a clan name as a campsite suggests that the Israelites passed through territory associated with this particular desert people. The wilderness journey was not through absolute wasteland but through territory occupied by various semi-nomadic peoples whose identity was embedded in the landscape.
32: From Bene-jaakan to Hor-haggidgad. Hor-haggidgad may mean 'cavern of Gidgad' or 'mountain-cave' — a rocky site. Deuteronomy 10:7 mentions Gudgodah and Jotbath (the following stage), suggesting a route through the Arabah or the southern Negeb approaching Edom. The Deut 10:6-7 sequence preserves a memory of these same stages in a slightly different order, reflecting the complexity of harmonizing different early sources about the wilderness route.
33: From Hor-haggidgad to Jotbathah. Deuteronomy 10:7 describes Jotbath as 'a land of streams of water' — a notable oasis in a dry region. The contrast between water-rich and waterless stages is a recurring theme of the wilderness journey. Provision was not uniform: some stages brought abundance, others brought scarcity, and the community's response to each tested their trust in YHWH's ongoing care.
34: From Jotbathah to Abronah. Abronah appears only here and in v.35 — unknown outside the itinerary. The name may be related to the word ever (crossing, passage) — possibly a crossing-point over a wadi or water feature. The Arabah route toward Ezion-geber (v.35) involved several such natural obstacles in the desert terrain.
35: From Abronah to Ezion-geber. Ezion-geber is a known site — the port city at the northern end of the Gulf of Aqaba (modern-day Eilat/Aqaba area). In later history it was Solomon's shipbuilding base (1 Kgs 9:26; 2 Chr 8:17) and an important Red Sea trade terminus. The Israelites' campsite at Ezion-geber brings them to the southern end of the Arabah, approaching Kadesh from the south and east.
36: From Ezion-geber to the wilderness of Zin — 'that is, Kadesh.' The identification of Kadesh with the wilderness of Zin is explicit here. Kadesh-barnea is the central wilderness campsite for the second half of Israel's 40 years: after the spy failure they returned and remained in the region of Kadesh for an extended period before moving north toward Canaan. Kadesh marks both the failure (the spy report, Num 13-14) and the beginning of the end (the entry into the final approach to Canaan). The qualification 'that is, Kadesh' reflects editorial clarity — ensuring the reader connects the itinerary's geographic notation to the well-known covenant location.
37: From Kadesh to Mount Hor, 'on the border of Edom.' Mount Hor is the final campsite before Aaron's death (vv.38-39) and the beginning of the northward march toward Canaan. The Edom border is significant: Edom refused Israel passage through its territory (Num 20:14-21), forcing the detour south through the Arabah. The border location establishes the geopolitical context: Israel is approaching Canaan from the east, navigating between Edom and Moab.
38: 'Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at the LORD's command and died there in the fortieth year after the Israelites came out of Egypt, on the first day of the fifth month.' The itinerary pauses its formulaic listing for an unusually precise death notice. The date (1 Av, fortieth year) is given with patriarchal precision — the same calendrical specificity used for Moses's age at death (Deut 31:2; 34:7) and for the Passover departure (v.3). Aaron's death is a covenant milestone that deserves exact dating. The phrase al pi YHWH (at the LORD's command) frames the death as divinely ordered, not accidental.
39: 'Aaron was 123 years old when he died at Mount Hor.' The age is given exactly. Aaron was three years older than Moses (Exod 7:7: Moses was 80, Aaron 83 at the Exodus). At the death notice — 40 years later — Aaron is 123. The precision of the death record (date + age) places Aaron in the lineage of patriarchs whose ages and deaths are carefully recorded: Adam (930), Noah (950), Abraham (175), Isaac (180), Jacob (147), Moses (120). Aaron's 123 years complete the sequence of the wilderness generation's leadership, marking the transition from Aaron to Eleazar and from one era of priestly service to the next.
40: 'The Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negeb in Canaan, heard that the Israelites were approaching.' A brief military intelligence notation embedded in the itinerary — the king of Arad appears in Num 21:1-3 as well, where Israel defeated him and devoted his cities to destruction (Hormah). The itinerary's inclusion of this geopolitical notice shows that the march document contained more than campsite names: it recorded the political-military context of Israel's approach to Canaan, noting who was aware of their movements.
41: From Mount Hor to Zalmonah. The journey continues north after Aaron's death. Zalmonah may relate to tzelem (shadow/image) or the place-name Zalmon. The stage after Aaron's death is recorded with the same formulaic notation as any other — the itinerary does not editorialize, but the reader feels the weight: Israel continues moving toward Canaan even as its founding priest is buried behind them.
42: From Zalmonah to Punon. Punon is a known site — the copper-mining district of Feinan in modern Jordan, where significant ancient slag heaps have been found. Archaeological surveys of Feinan reveal extensive Bronze Age copper-smelting activity: Israel camped in an industrial region with major metallurgical operations. The Copper Scroll from Qumran mentions Punon; the site's copper resources were exploited continuously from the Early Bronze Age through the Byzantine period.
43: From Punon to Oboth. Oboth appears also in Num 21:10-11, where Israel camped after the bronze serpent episode (Num 21:4-9). The itinerary and the Num 21 narrative account for the same stages but in slightly different sequences — a known textual complexity suggesting that the two accounts draw on related but independent traditions about the Transjordan approach.
44: From Oboth to Iye-abarim, 'on Moab's border.' The name Iye-abarim means 'ruins of the Abarim' — on the eastern edge of the Abarim mountain range (the same range where Moses will later view Canaan and die, 27:12). The Moab border is significant: Israel is skirting around Moabite territory, having been denied transit through both Edom (Num 20:14-21) and Moab (Num 22-25 implies Moab's hostility). The approach to Canaan required navigating between hostile neighbors on both sides.
45: From Iyim to Dibon-gad. Dibon (mentioned as a Gadite settlement in 32:3, 34) is here qualified as 'Dibon-gad' — already associated with the Gadite claim. The name anticipates the settlement of ch.32: Dibon is already being identified with Gad even before the formal allotment. The itinerary and the settlement narrative of ch.32 are in dialogue: the march route passes through territories that will become specific tribal inheritances.
46: From Dibon-gad to Almon-diblathaim. Almon-diblathaim means 'hidden place of the two fig-cakes' — a possibly agricultural place-name. The site may correspond to Beth-diblathaim mentioned in Jer 48:22 in an oracle against Moab, suggesting a location in the Moabite plateau region north of the Arnon.
47: From Almon-diblathaim to the mountains of Abarim, 'before Nebo.' Mount Nebo is where Moses will die and view Canaan (Deut 32:49; 34:1-4). The itinerary brings Israel to the foot of the mountain whose summit will be Moses's last view of the promised land. The camp 'before Nebo' — al penei nevoh — is Israel's penultimate wilderness position: they are within sight of the death-mountain of their leader and within sight of the land across the Jordan.
48: From the Abarim mountains to the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho. The plains of Moab (arvot moav) are the final campsite — Israel's position in the last chapters of Numbers, Deuteronomy, and the entry point to Joshua. The Jordan 'at Jericho' establishes the crossing point: Jericho is the first Canaanite city to be attacked. The itinerary's final stage brings the 40-year journey to its destination: the Jordan crossing is now geographically imminent.
49: 'Their camp stretched from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim in the plains of Moab.' The camp's dimensions are given: from Beth-jeshimoth (house of desolation/wastes) in the south to Abel-shittim (meadow of acacias) in the north. Abel-shittim is where the Baal-Peor episode occurred (Num 25:1) — the final major failure before the Canaan crossing. The itinerary records the geographic extent of the final camp: the community is massive enough that its campsite spans a substantial distance along the Jordan bank.
50: 'The LORD spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho.' The final revelation formula of the itinerary section. The geographic specificity — plains of Moab, by the Jordan, at Jericho — is repeated from v.48: this is the recognized covenantal locus for the final wilderness legislation. The Sinai formula was 'in the wilderness of Sinai'; the plains of Moab formula is the corresponding terminal formula for the legislation of the wilderness period's end.
51: 'Tell the Israelites: When you cross the Jordan into Canaan.' The conditional formula launches the Canaan-entry legislation. Ki atam ovrim et-hayyarden el-eretz knaan — 'for you are crossing the Jordan into the land of Canaan.' The use of the participial form (ovrim) gives the action an imminent, almost present force: the crossing is as good as happening. The instruction addresses the post-crossing situation: what Israel must do immediately upon entering the land.
52: 'Drive out all the land's inhabitants, smash all their carved stones, destroy all their cast metal images, and demolish all their high places.' Three categories of destruction are specified: maskiyot (carved stones — standing stones or incised stone reliefs associated with Canaanite worship), tzalme massekot (cast metal images — molded divine figures), and bamot (high places — elevated outdoor sanctuaries). Archaeological evidence from Canaanite sites (Megiddo, Hazor, Lachish) confirms the prevalence of all three: standing stones (masseboth), bronze figurines, and hilltop sanctuaries were standard features of Late Bronze Age Canaanite religious practice. The command is total: no accommodation, no partial retention.
53: 'Take possession of the land and live in it, for I have given it to you.' The positive counterpart to the negative dispossession command. V'horashtem et ha'aretz v'yishavtem bah ki lakhem natati et ha'aretz lareshet otah — three verbs: possess, live, receive. The land is YHWH's gift; Israel's task is to receive it through the act of taking possession. The gift-language counters any sense that the military dispossession is an act of pure human aggression: Israel is receiving what YHWH has given, not seizing what they have no claim to.
54: 'Distribute the land by lot among your clans — larger groups receive larger allotments, smaller groups smaller.' V'hitchalkaltem et ha'aretz b'goral — distribute by lot. The lot (goral) is a mechanism of divine guidance: the distribution is not decided by human negotiation or political power but by a divination process that places the outcome in YHWH's hands. The proportionality principle (larger groups get more) applies divine equity: the lot is not a fixed geography but a method by which the land's total area is proportionally apportioned to each clan. The division of Canaan into tribal allotments (Josh 13-19) will execute this command precisely.
55: 'But if you do not drive out the land's inhabitants, those you leave will be thorns in your eyes and splinters in your sides.' L'tzinim b'einekhem ulshkokim batzidekhem — thorns in the eyes, splinters in the sides. The anatomical metaphor is visceral: the pain of the remaining Canaanites will be intimate and inescapable, not distant and abstract. This warning is prophetic: Judges will document exactly this pattern — Israel fails to drive out the Canaanites, and the remaining inhabitants become a source of religious compromise and military pressure. The warning's fulfillment is the history of the judges period.
56: 'And what I had planned to do to them, I will do to you.' The covenant threat: the same dispossession judgment that was to fall on Canaan's inhabitants for their covenant-violating practices will fall on Israel if Israel adopts those same practices. The logic is Lev 18:24-28 applied prospectively: the land vomits out those who defile it, regardless of ethnic identity. Israel has no inherent immunity from the divine judgment that drives out the wicked from YHWH's land. The chapter ends with the itinerary completed and the covenant stakes fully stated: the land is waiting, the conditions are clear, and the consequence of failure is exactly as severe as the consequence for those who occupied the land before Israel.
